revision_reviewed: rev-5 with rev-3 errata (plan as of 2026-05-12 uncommitted edits)
narrative:
The rev-3 B1 fix replaced the ad-hoc path.split() loop with value_provider extraction. This closes the container-descent skip. However the post-fix assertion body (lines 1598-1602) introduces a `isinstance(raw_value, str) and raw_value` filter that silently skips the entire assertion for any Sensitive[T] where T is not a non-empty string. Task 15 explicitly plans `Annotated[dict[str, Any], Sensitive(summarizer=...)]` for patch_*_options (plan line 1288), and Task 8 handles summarizer paths for bytes values in blob tools. The property test as written will never execute an assertion line for these annotation classes. The structural shape is identical to B1: a load-bearing security property test that passes vacuously for entire known annotation classes. The closure claim for rev-2 BLOCKER_A survives only for flat-dict string fields; it collapses the moment a non-string Sensitive[T] enters the manifest.

Two other residual modes compound this: (a) the `if extracted is None: continue` guard at line 1581 silently skips Optional[Annotated[str, Sensitive()]] when Hypothesis generates None — this is exactly the arm plan-review W8 lists as a known coverage gap; the property test should be the net that catches leaks in that arm, but instead it skips it. (b) The exception-swallowing fallback at lines 1591-1594 (`except (TypeError, ValueError): raw_values = [extracted]`) is structurally the same defensive pattern that caused B1: if value_provider for a container-descent path returns an unexpected shape, the test silently degrades to scalar treatment rather than failing loudly.

The oracle fix (S2/M1) remains partial. Task 10 floor-check uses walk_model_schema itself as the oracle — the iterator is its own witness. Rev-3 M1 asked for a separate-codepath oracle on a fixture model covering all four annotation forms. The floor-check catches top-level field omissions but does not separate shared-iterator blind spots.

The call-order test (S3/M2) is a genuine improvement: it pins that redact_tool_call_arguments raises AuditIntegrityError for an unknown tool name. This mechanically constrains Phase 3's call ordering. The caveat is that it tests a property of the redaction function, not a property of Phase 3's call site — Phase 3 could catch AuditIntegrityError and continue. Given that CLAUDE.md explicitly prohibits such catch-and-continue for system code, this is acceptable. Marked CLOSED with caveat.

S6 (N7 unconditional _TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS removal) remains conditional at plan line 1260. The rev-3 N7 ask was to make the removal unconditional. Not addressed.

The N6/S5 bulk-editorial saturation risk is acknowledged in documentation but no mechanical throttle exists. The label-gate still clears an arbitrary number of simultaneous weakenings with a single check.

blocking_issues.title: Property test `isinstance(raw_value, str)` filter silently skips all non-string Sensitive[T] annotations
blocking_issues.defect: Line 1598: `if isinstance(raw_value, str) and raw_value:` — this filter causes the assertion body (lines 1599-1602) to never execute for Sensitive[T] where T is dict, bytes, int, list, or any non-string type. Task 15 explicitly plans `Annotated[dict[str, Any], Sensitive(summarizer=...)]` for patch_*_options (plan line 1288); blob tools use bytes-adjacent types. The test passes vacuously for this entire annotation class. This is structurally identical to the rev-3 B1 defect: a load-bearing security property test that silently skips known annotation classes.
blocking_issues.vacuous_pass_example: When Hypothesis generates payload.options = {'api_key': 'secret'} for a Sensitive[dict[str,Any]] field: extracted = {'api_key': 'secret'}; isinstance({'api_key': 'secret'}, str) is False; loop body skipped; no assertion runs; test passes regardless of whether redaction worked.
blocking_issues.also_affected: Empty-string values: `isinstance(raw_value, str) and raw_value` skips empty strings; Hypothesis routinely generates empty strings from st.from_type(str); these pass vacuously.
why_blocking: Cited closure mechanism for rev-2 BLOCKER_A (plan Appendix B, Appendix C)
Listed in Phase 2 Done When (plan lines 1716-1717)
Phase 3 commits to this test's correctness claim
Failure mode is silent (passing test, false confidence)
Task 15 and Task 8 introduce exactly the annotation classes the filter skips
blocking_issues.resolution: Replace the filter with type-appropriate serialization checks. For str: `isinstance(raw_value, str) and raw_value`. For non-str types: serialize `raw_value` with json.dumps (using a fallback encoder) and check whether the serialized string appears in `serialized`. Alternatively, expand the assertion to: `assert str(raw_value) not in serialized or raw_value == REDACTED_SENTINEL_VALUE`. The safest fix is to check the serialized form of the value regardless of type, not just strings.

secondary_mode_1.defect: `if extracted is None: continue` silently skips Optional[Annotated[str, Sensitive()]] when Hypothesis generates None. Plan-review W8 identifies this arm as a known coverage gap. The property test should catch leaks for this arm, but instead silently skips it.
secondary_mode_1.resolution: When extracted is None the field was not set; this is safe. However the test should assert that None is either (a) not an annotation-class that ever produces non-None sensitive data, or (b) the skip is bounded by a separate test for the Optional arm.

secondary_mode_2.defect: `except (TypeError, ValueError): raw_values = [extracted]` silently degrades to scalar treatment when the container-descent unpacking fails. If value_provider returns an unexpected shape, the test does not raise — it silently treats the whole container as the raw value. This is structurally the same defensive fallback that caused B1.
secondary_mode_2.resolution: Remove the except clause or make it re-raise. If value_provider returns an unexpected shape, that is a test-setup bug that must fail loudly, not silently degrade.

major.title: Walker completeness floor-check is not a separate-codepath oracle
major.issue: Rev-3 M1 asked for a separate-codepath oracle for currently-supported annotation forms (Annotated[scalar], Annotated[list], Annotated[dict], Optional[Annotated[scalar]]) asserting the iterator yields expected nodes on a fixture model. The plan instead added `test_walker_emits_node_for_every_top_level_field` which calls `walk_model_schema(entry.argument_model)` against production MANIFEST entries. The iterator is its own oracle. Two structural weaknesses: (1) only top-level field roots are checked, not container-descent paths; (2) a shared annotation-form bug that silently drops a node still results in `actual` not containing that root, but the test only checks `expected <= actual` for `model_fields.keys()` — it does not check that Sensitive nodes are correctly emitted for each annotation form. A fixture model covering all four annotation forms with known-expected Sensitive node paths would separate the codepaths.
major.status: PARTIAL — floor-check improvement is real; separate-codepath oracle requirement is not met

warnings.title: Property test does not assert sensitive_nodes is non-empty
warnings.issue: The parametrize decorator filters to manifest entries with `entry.argument_model is not None` (line 1552). But for a given model, `sensitive_nodes` (line 1573) may be empty if walk_model_schema yields zero Sensitive-marked nodes — either by spec (a type-driven model with no Sensitive fields) or by iterator bug. When sensitive_nodes is empty the inner loop is vacuous. The test should assert `len(sensitive_nodes) > 0` for models that are expected to have Sensitive fields, or the parametrize set should be restricted to manifest entries with at least one Sensitive annotation.
warnings.severity: Warning — same vacuous-pass risk shape as B1, lower likelihood because most type-driven entries are promoted precisely because they have Sensitive fields

warnings.title: No Done-When criterion for summarizer_errors_total SLO assertion in telemetry config
warnings.issue: Phase 2 Done When item: `composer.redaction.summarizer_errors_total SLO threshold is asserted to be 0 in production telemetry config (RSK-03)`. The plan does not identify which file this assertion lives in or which task creates it. The telemetry integration test in Task 19 checks that counters fire in an integration scenario but does not assert the SLO = 0 constraint in production config. Without a mechanical enforcement point, a counter that fires will have no alarm. CLAUDE.md: 'No silent failures — every telemetry emission point must send or explicitly acknowledge nothing to send' — the inverse is that a counter firing above zero must surface somewhere.
warnings.severity: Warning — not blocking, but the Done-When criterion is currently unaddressed in any task

warnings.title: Snapshot divergence on parallel feature-branch merges has no deterministic resolution
warnings.issue: The policy-hash snapshot is a committed JSON file. If two feature branches both modify MANIFEST entries and both merge to main at similar times, the second merge will overwrite the first's snapshot entries for unchanged tools. Because the snapshot is bootstrapped by script (idempotent per plan), the resolution is to re-run bootstrap_redaction_snapshot.py post-merge. But this is documentary, not mechanical — the plan does not require the bootstrap script to run as a merge-time CI step, only as a pre-commit task in Task 16/19. A stale snapshot silently fails the adequacy guard test rather than flagging a merge-ordering problem.
warnings.severity: Warning — recovery process is clear (re-run bootstrap), but the failure mode is a silent test failure rather than a loud diagnostic

minor.title: _TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS removal remains conditional
minor.issue: Plan line 1260: 'If `_TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS[tool]` is now redundant, remove it; otherwise document why both are kept.' Rev-3 N7 asked this to be made unconditional (Pydantic model with extra=forbid is strictly more capable; no justification for retaining the old structure). Still conditional in rev-4. Mechanical enforcement gap: a promoted tool could silently keep both the Pydantic guard AND the old required-paths guard, creating two error paths for the same validation, with different error classes (complicates audit trail interpretation).

minor.title: Bulk-editorial PR clears arbitrary number of simultaneous weakenings with single label-gate check
minor.issue: Label-gate (Task 18) sums sensitive_path_count across all changed entries. A single `policy-weaken-justified` label and a single rationale section in the PR body clears the gate regardless of how many tools have their Sensitive coverage reduced. Rev-3 N6 flagged this as a known limitation. Plan Appendix documentation acknowledges it but no per-entry floor or throttle mechanism is proposed. Accepted limitation per plan, but documented here as ongoing systems risk.
minor.status: OPEN — documented known limitation, not newly introduced by rev-4

minor.title: Rev-2 m_dynamic_tool_name warning comment not verifiable
minor.issue: Rev-3 N5 flagged that `m_dynamic_tool_name` deserves a warning comment in MANIFEST construction. This is not surfaced in any task step or Done-When criterion. Appendix B closure table does not reference it.
minor.status: OPEN as minor — low risk but untracked

S1_property_test_pathskip.evidence: Rev-3 B1 fix (plan Appendix C, Task 19 lines 1519-1605) correctly replaces ad-hoc path.split() with value_provider. The container-descent skip mode that caused B1 is closed. However, the post-fix assertion body at line 1598 introduces `isinstance(raw_value, str) and raw_value` which silently skips the assertion for any non-string Sensitive[T]. Task 15 (plan line 1288) explicitly introduces Sensitive[dict[str,Any]] annotations. The test passes vacuously for that entire annotation class. The structural shape — a load-bearing security property test that silently skips known annotation classes — is identical to B1. This is a new B1-class defect introduced by the rev-3 fix, not a remnant of the original. Additionally: `if extracted is None: continue` (line 1581) silently skips Optional[Annotated[str, Sensitive()]], and `except (TypeError, ValueError): raw_values = [extracted]` (lines 1591-1594) silently degrades on unexpected value_provider output. S1 is not CLOSED.

S2_iterator_completeness_oracle.evidence: Task 10 `test_walker_emits_node_for_every_top_level_field` (plan lines 1173-1198) is a real improvement: it asserts every model_fields key appears as a walk root. However, rev-3 M1 specifically asked for a separate-codepath oracle on a fixture model covering all four annotation forms (Annotated[scalar], Annotated[list], Annotated[dict], Optional[Annotated[scalar]]). The plan uses walk_model_schema itself as the oracle against production MANIFEST entries — the iterator is still its own witness for annotation-form coverage. Container-descent paths are not separately floor-checked. Marking PARTIAL rather than OPEN because the top-level floor-check is a genuine improvement; the oracle separation requirement is not met.

S3_call_order_precondition.evidence: Task 17 (plan lines 1364-1391) adds `test_redact_tool_call_arguments_raises_for_unknown_tool` which asserts AuditIntegrityError is raised for a tool name absent from MANIFEST. This is a 5-line mechanical test that pins the precondition. The test addresses the CLAUDE.md principle: 'if a constraint isn't mechanically enforced, assume the next session won't know about it.' Caveat: the test pins a property of the redaction function (it raises), not a property of Phase 3's call site ordering (Phase 3 could in principle catch AuditIntegrityError). Given CLAUDE.md's explicit prohibition on catch-and-continue for system code, this caveat is acceptable but should be documented in the test.

S4_rev2_minor_closure_tracking.evidence: Appendix B (plan lines 1751-1765) provides a rev-2 finding closure cross-reference table. Five rev-2 minors are listed. m_dynamic_tool_name is not explicitly addressed in any task or Done-When criterion — it appears in plan-review N5 (rev-3) but no task step explicitly adds a warning comment to MANIFEST construction for dynamic tool names. The other four rev-2 minors (m_script_dir_missing, m_citation_hygiene, sorted_non_string_keys, double_json_parse) are clearly addressed.

S5_bulk_editorial_pr_label_clearance.evidence: Task 18 direction-aware label gate (plan lines 1397-1498) correctly distinguishes weaken vs strengthen direction and requires a rationale section. However the gate clears an arbitrary number of simultaneous entry weakenings with a single label check and a single PR-body rationale section. No per-entry floor, no maximum-weakening-count throttle. Plan documents this in docs/guides/redaction-policy-changes.md (plan line 1496) as a governance note. The limitation is known and acknowledged but there is no mechanical mitigation.

S6_tool_required_paths_unconditional_rem.evidence: Plan line 1260 retains the conditional phrasing: 'If `_TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS[tool]` is now redundant, remove it; otherwise document why both are kept.' Rev-3 N7 asked for unconditional removal (the Pydantic model with extra=forbid is strictly more capable). The conditionality was not removed in rev-4. Risk: promoted tools could silently retain both validation paths, producing different error classes (MissingRequiredPaths vs ValidationError/ToolArgumentError) from the same input, complicating audit trail interpretation per plan N3 (audit consumer impact).

new_risks_from_rev4_expansion.description: The ~146-line rev-3 expansion (Appendix C + walker floor-check + call-order test) does not introduce ordering risks for earlier tasks. All new content is additive: Task 10 adds a test, Task 17 adds a test, Appendix C adds closure records. No earlier task gains a new precondition documented only in a later task. No new ordering risk identified.

dependency_chain_assessment.failure_mode_independence: The five controls (runtime walker, adequacy guard, policy hash snapshot, direction-aware label gate, Hypothesis property test) share the iterator as a common dependency. The walker floor-check breaks the shared-iterator blind spot for top-level fields but not for annotation-form coverage. If walk_model_schema silently mishandles Sensitive[dict[str,Any]], both the adequacy guard (uses same iterator) and the property test (filters out non-str values) will pass without detecting the omission. The tracer-bullet end-to-end test (Task 6) is the only control that does not depend on walk_model_schema — it calls redact_tool_call_arguments directly and asserts canary-absent from output. However it covers only `set_source`, not the Sensitive[dict] annotation class. Independent failure remains limited to the snapshot and label-gate layer.
dependency_chain_assessment.risk_level: Medium — single annotation-class blind spot not caught by multiple controls simultaneously

rev3_closures.S1: OPEN (new B1-class defect introduced by rev-3 fix)

summary.one_line: The rev-3 B1 fix replaces one vacuous-pass mode with another: the value_provider rewrite is correct, but the isinstance(raw_value, str) filter at line 1598 silently skips the assertion for all non-string Sensitive[T] annotations, including the Sensitive[dict[str,Any]] type that Task 15 introduces. The structural shape — a load-bearing security property test that passes vacuously for entire annotation classes — is identical to the original B1.

notes: The test at lines 1554-1604 is structured as an outer parametrize over tool names and an inner @given/@settings Hypothesis test (check()). The value_provider rewrite between lines 1567-1603 is correct for the container-descent path problem. The fix is real. The new defect is narrower in scope than B1 (only non-string Sensitive[T] are affected, and only type-driven entries are parametrized) but it matters because Task 15 explicitly plans the affected annotation class. Resolution is a small target change at lines 1597-1602: handle serialized-repr comparison for non-str types rather than filtering them out. The secondary modes (None skip at 1581, except clause at 1591-1594) should be fixed in the same pass.
